### [AKT Signaling Pathway](https://www.thermofisher.com/us/en/home/life-science/antibodies/antibodies-learning-center/antibodies-resource-library/cell-signaling-pathways/akt-signaling-pathway.html)

AKT is a serine/threonine kinase that is involved in mediating various biological responses, such as inhibition of apoptosis.

SUMMARY:
Similar to the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway, multiple studies have clearly demonstrated the presence of activated Ras/Raf/MEK/ERK in ovarian cancers.2,8,9,13 Various molecular components of this chain work through successive phosphorylation and subsequent activation of each downstream target which ultimately activate ERK1 and ERK2, otherwise known as mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK).8 MAPK in turn produces multiple cytosolic and nuclear substrates through mRNA transcription and translation, which ultimately serve as the backbone for cell growth and proliferation.13 As such, hyperactivation of this pathway has been shown to be associated with specific mutations in BRAF and KRAS proteins.8 Not surprisingly, some of the most frequent genetic abnormalities in ovarian cancers are mutations in these proteins.

### Major signaling pathways

Key signaling pathways conserved across many multicellular organisms and present in different cell types have been well described in the literature. Canonical, well-known signaling pathways currently provide the basis of our knowledge about underlying mechanisms of function of separate cells, tissues, and organs. A [signaling pathway](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/signal-transduction "Learn more about signaling pathway from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), therefore, is a simplified model of complex [molecular interactions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/molecular-interaction "Learn more about molecular interactions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that take place inside a living cell. This terminology is clarified in the succeeding text. In live cells, signaling cascades largely interconnect so that it is hard to separate one pathway from another and it is difficult to describe the entire network of [biochemical processes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/biochemical-process "Learn more about biochemical processes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") as little is known about some of them. However, it does make sense to try to separate distinctive pathways. Often, these distinctive pathways are the fastest and most straightforward signaling routes, which can be easily observed experimentally. Changes in the activity of proteins in the model pathway can be used as clear indicators of a cellular response to a specific signal, for example, when a drug is added to cultured cells.

Major signaling pathways include, but are not limited, to the WNT, SHH, notch pathways and the [MAPK](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/mitogen-activated-protein-kinase "Learn more about MAPK from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), RAS, [mTOR](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/mammalian-target-of-rapamycin "Learn more about mTOR from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), JAK-STAT, and NF-kB cascades.

[MAPK signaling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/mapk-signaling "Learn more about MAPK signaling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is activated by many incoming signals, and it plays an essential role in many [cellular processes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/cellular-processes "Learn more about cellular processes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Generally, MAPK, a mitogen-activated [protein kinase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/protein-kinases "Learn more about protein kinase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), signaling cascade starts with the phosphorylation of MAP3 kinases and ends with the phosphorylation of nuclear transcription factors by MAP kinases. Signal flow in the MAPK cascade usually has only one direction: from MAP3K to MAPK. It is also known that MAP kinases of the same order do not phosphorylate each other (Kim and Choi, 2010; Pearson et al., 2001).

RAS subfamily signaling is composed of a group of signaling pathways where the central role belongs to [small GTPase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/small-gtpase "Learn more about small GTPase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") proteins of the RAS subfamily (e.g., [HRAS](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/hras "Learn more about HRAS from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [KRAS](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/kras "Learn more about KRAS from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and NRAS). Those are intracellular proteins that are activated by different incoming signals and pass the signal to other proteins involved in regulating the transcription of genes associated with growth, differentiation, and survival of a cell. These cellular processes are always impaired in tumor cells, which makes studying the RAS subfamily of proteins an important area of cancer research (Goldfinger, 2008; Rocks et al., 2006).

[mTOR signaling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/mtor-signaling "Learn more about mTOR signaling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is a group of signaling pathways featuring mTOR, [mechanistic target of rapamycin kinase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/mammalian-target-of-rapamycin "Learn more about mechanistic target of rapamycin kinase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), serine-threonine protein kinase, and protein. This protein as part of two protein complexes, mTORC1 and mTORC2, regulates processes critical for cell survival including growth, proliferation, [protein synthesis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/protein-biosynthesis "Learn more about protein synthesis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), autophagy, and the maintenance of [cytoskeleton](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/cytoskeleton "Learn more about cytoskeleton from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") structure. mTOR signaling is actively studied in the fields of diabetes, cancer, and aging (Crino, 2015; Zeng and Chi, 2014).

JAK-STAT signaling is composed of a group of signaling pathways characterized by conservative Janus kinase (JAK) proteins. JAKs are activated by cell [surface receptors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/surface-receptor "Learn more about surface receptors from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that themselves dimerize after ligand binding (such as interleukins receptors), bringing different [JAK](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/janus-kinase "Learn more about JAK from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") molecules physically close to each other and allowing them to transphosphorylate each other leading to activation. Activated JAK enzymes phosphorylate [tyrosine](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/tyrosine "Learn more about tyrosine from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") residues in their receptors that in turn attracts nuclear transcription factors of the signal transducer and activator of transcription (STAT) family due to the affinity of phosphorylated tyrosine (phosphotyrosine) in JAKs to the src homology 2 (SH2) domains of the STATs. Then, JAKs phosphorylate tyrosine residues in [STAT proteins](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/stat-protein "Learn more about STAT proteins from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), dissociating STATs from the receptors, so that STAT proteins can form [homodimers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/homodimer "Learn more about homodimers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). STAT homodimers in turn penetrate the nucleus and activate the transcription of multiple genes involved in the recruitment and survival of [immune cells](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/immunocompetent-cell "Learn more about immune cells from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (Kiu and Nicholson, 2012; Nicolas et al., 2013).

WNT signaling is performed by a group of signaling pathways discovered in all animals. It is named after a specific ligand—proteins of the WNT, wingless-type [MMTV](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/mouse-mammary-tumor-virus "Learn more about MMTV from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") integration site, family. WNT proteins undergo different modifications during their processing; the most important one is [acylation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/acylation "Learn more about acylation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that facilitates transport of WNT to cell membrane. WNT ligands bind to specific [cell surface receptors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/cell-surface-receptor "Learn more about cell surface receptors from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") including [Frizzled](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/frizzled "Learn more about Frizzled from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (FZD) and the receptors [LRP5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/lrp5 "Learn more about LRP5 from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and LRP6 (LDL receptor–related proteins 5 and 6). [Intracellular signaling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/intracellular-signaling "Learn more about Intracellular signaling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the WNT/FZD pathway can go several ways. Canonical signaling leads to stabilization of the [cytoplasmic protein](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/cytoplasm-protein "Learn more about cytoplasmic protein from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") beta-catenin that accumulates and eventually gets into nucleus to interact with the transcription factor T-cell factor (TCF) and thus influence the expression of genes involved in the control of [cell proliferation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cell-proliferation "Learn more about cell proliferation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This is especially true in multipotent cells. Noncanonical (beta-catenin independent) WNT signaling begins with the binding of WNT with the receptor-like [tyrosine kinase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/tyrosine-kinase "Learn more about tyrosine kinase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (RYK) and the [retinoic acid receptor](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/retinoic-acid-receptor "Learn more about retinoic acid receptor from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (RAR)–related [orphan receptor](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/orphan-receptor "Learn more about orphan receptor from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (ROR) proteins, which control [cell polarity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cell-polarity "Learn more about cell polarity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and play a role in [morphogenesis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/morphogenesis "Learn more about morphogenesis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (Chen et al., 2008; Komiya and Habas, 2008).

[Hedgehog signaling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/hedgehog-signaling-pathway "Learn more about Hedgehog signaling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is found in all animals with bilateral symmetry. It is based on interactions between the ligands sonic hedgehog (SHH), Indian hedgehog (IHH), desert hedgehog (DHH), and the [receptors patched](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/protein-patched "Learn more about receptors patched from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PTCH1 and PTCH2) and smoothened (SMO). Binding of ligands to receptors leads to activation of SMO that is transported into the cytoplasm where it inhibits the [SUFU](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/sufu "Learn more about SUFU from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") protein, a negative regulator of hedgehog signaling. This in turn leads to activation of the GLI family of [zinc finger](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/zinc-finger-protein "Learn more about zinc finger from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") 1 (GLI1) transcription factors and initiation of the transcription of genes responsible for embryonal development and cell proliferation. Impairment of Hedgehog signaling is found to promote [angiogenesis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/angiogenesis "Learn more about angiogenesis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [tumor metastasis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/metastatic-carcinoma "Learn more about tumor metastasis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (Jacob and Lum, 2007).

Nuclear factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cell (NF-kB) is a [protein complex](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/protein-complexes "Learn more about protein complex from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of transcription factors that exists in almost all animal cells. It initiates [gene transcription](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetic-transcription "Learn more about gene transcription from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in response to various external stimuli including stress, cytokines, pathogens, and other dangers like [reactive oxygen species](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/reactive-oxygen-species "Learn more about reactive oxygen species from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [heavy metals](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/heavy-metal "Learn more about heavy metals from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). NF-kB plays a critical role in the immune response, and its involvement in synaptic plasticity and memory was shown as well. The NF-kB complex consists of the proteins [NFKB1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/nfkb1 "Learn more about NFKB1 from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [NFKB2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/nfkb2 "Learn more about NFKB2 from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), REL, [RELA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/rela "Learn more about RELA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and RELB. They should be considered together with their regulatory counterparts—NFKB inhibitor alpha (NFKBIA)—that block NFKB1 and that can be itself blocked by proteins of the inhibitor of kappa-light [polypeptide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/polypeptide "Learn more about polypeptide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") gene enhancer family (IKBKB, [IKBKG](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/ikbkg "Learn more about IKBKG from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), IKBKE, and IKBKAP) or by the conserved helix-loop-helix ubiquitous kinase (CHUK). The canonical NF-kB signaling pathway implies activation of this system of dual inhibitors via MAPK signaling cascade that results in the activation of transcription of genes involved in the inflammatory response, cell growth, and the block of [apoptosis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/programmed-cell-death "Learn more about apoptosis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). A noncanonical pathway was only discovered in [cells of the immune system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/cells-of-the-immune-system "Learn more about cells of the immune system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and it is associated with activation of the NF-kB complex through the MAP3K14/CHUCK [phosphorylation cascade](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/phosphorylation-cascade "Learn more about phosphorylation cascade from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). For example, this initiates transcription of ICAM1 and CXCL12 leading to B-cell differentiation (Gilmore, 2006; Hoffmann et al., 2006).

In this chapter, we briefly define several well-studied signaling cascades; however, the diversity of signaling pathways is not limited to those described earlier. With that said, initiation and the sequence of events in many known cell processes include these signaling pathways. It is worth mentioning that research of most known signaling pathways is generally focused on studying pathological states of organism or it uses cell culture models. There is very little information in the literature about how [cell signaling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/signal-transduction "Learn more about cell signaling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") pathways work under normal physiological conditions.

### 5 Future perspective

The concept of cell signaling pathways has been widely accepted by people who have never stopped studying signaling pathways, but at present no one can say for sure that each kind of signaling pathway is well-understood. Therefore, in the process of exploring signaling pathways, several aspects should be considered: ① the influence of mixed [nanomaterials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/nanomaterial "Learn more about nanomaterials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") on the signal pathway after entering the organism should be considered. In fact, the nanomaterials in contact with organisms in life are not just a single one. When a variety of different types of [nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/nanoparticle "Learn more about nanoparticles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") enter the body in different ways, whether it will cause the disorder of multiple signal pathways, or if some pathways has positive and negative feedback to a certain pathway should be considered as further study. ② Whether the signal pathways studied in model organisms are also applicable to non-model organisms. Model organisms have always been the research object of various experiments. One of the reasons is that human have a comprehensive understanding of all aspects of model organisms, but whether the signal pathways obtained from model organisms are fully consistent when extrapolated to other organisms is also a major challenge for future research. ③ Certain research should also be devoted to other biological macromolecules. The verification of signaling pathways is mostly focused on various proteins. Some biologically functional RNAs are often overlooked. To fully understand a signaling pathway, it cannot be limited to a single biological macromolecule. The development of nanotechnology aims to benefit human beings, there are also potential health risks. Therefore, only a thorough and detailed study of its toxicity mechanism can lay a safe and reliable foundation for the manufacture of related products.

### 3.10.1 Introduction: Cell-Signaling Pathways

Cell-signaling mechanisms describe the molecular events activated within cells to mediate growth, proliferation, differentiation, and survival. Those mechanisms are complex and only partially elucidated. Nevertheless [protein domains](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/protein-domain "Learn more about protein domains from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and interactions were identified and deregulation of those mechanisms was described leading to increased proliferation capacity, sustained [angiogenesis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/angiogenesis "Learn more about angiogenesis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), metastasis, as well as resistance to apoptosis. Most diseases present aberrations of cell-signaling processes and compounds that target disease-specific alterations of cell-signaling mechanisms are considered interesting compounds for future therapies. New [clinical trials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/clinical-trial "Learn more about clinical trials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have already generated a multitude of agents targeting cell-signaling pathways that are becoming increasingly complex to understand because of the highly tissue-specific nature of the signaling pathways.

Regulatory pathways are activated by extracellular factors, including hormones, growth factors, or cytokines. These extracellular factors activate intracellular cascades of protein networks within the cell. Whereas cell-signaling cascades demonstrate a high level of specificity, it becomes clear that many pathways are redundant and that inhibition of one specific pathway most often leads to the activation of parallel signaling pathways inducing activation of common targets involved in the development of diseases. Targeting highly specific pathways by single compounds inhibiting only one protein generally leads to resistance to this therapeutic agent.

### 4 Conclusion

[Cell signaling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/signal-transduction "Learn more about Cell signaling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") pathways play crucial role in AML LSCs maintenance and is a promising strategy to eliminate this cell population improving anti-AML therapy. Nevertheless, these signaling pathways are interconnected to regulate LSCs. The clinical use of functional assays based on LSCs, as well as molecular biomarkers, can be used to overcome these challenges and lead to better anti-AML treatment using pathway-based therapeutic strategies.

For the journal, see [Cellular Signalling](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cellular_Signalling "Cellular Signalling").

![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/f/f2/Edit-clear.svg/40px-Edit-clear.svg.png)This article's **[lead section](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Lead_section "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Lead section") may be too long**. Please read the [length guidelines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Lead_section#Length "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Lead section") and help [move details into the article's body](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Cell_signaling&action=edit)._(March 2025)_

In [biology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biology "Biology"), **cell signaling** (**cell signalling** in [British English](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_English "British English")) is the [process](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biological_process "Biological process") by which a [cell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") interacts with itself, other cells, and the environment. Cell signaling is a fundamental property of all [cellular life](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") in both [prokaryotes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prokaryote "Prokaryote") and [eukaryotes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote").

Typically, the signaling process involves three components: the signal, the receptor, and the effector.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]

In biology, signals are mostly chemical in nature, but can also be physical cues such as [pressure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pressure "Pressure"), [voltage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Membrane_potential "Membrane potential"), [temperature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Temperature "Temperature"), or light. Chemical signals are molecules with the ability to bind and activate a specific [receptor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Receptor_(biochemistry) "Receptor (biochemistry)"). These molecules, also referred to as [ligands](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ligand_(biochemistry) "Ligand (biochemistry)"), are chemically diverse, including [ions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ion "Ion") (e.g. Na+, K+, Ca 2+, etc.), lipids (e.g. steroid, prostaglandin), peptides (e.g. insulin, ACTH), carbohydrates, glycosylated proteins (proteoglycans), nucleic acids, etc. Peptide and lipid ligands are particularly important, as most hormones belong to these classes of chemicals. Peptides are usually polar, hydrophilic molecules. As such they are unable to diffuse freely across the bi-lipid layer of the plasma membrane, so their action is mediated by a cell membrane bound receptor. On the other hand, liposoluble chemicals such as steroid hormones, can diffuse passively across the plasma membrane and interact with intracellular receptors. Cell signaling can occur over short or long distances, and can be further classified as [autocrine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Autocrine "Autocrine"), [intracrine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intracrine "Intracrine"), [juxtacrine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Juxtacrine "Juxtacrine"), [paracrine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paracrine "Paracrine"), or [endocrine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Endocrine "Endocrine"). [Autocrine signaling](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Autocrine_signaling "Autocrine signaling") occurs when the chemical signal acts on the same cell that produced the signaling chemical.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_signaling#cite_note-1) Intracrine signaling occurs when the chemical signal produced by a cell acts on receptors located in the cytoplasm or nucleus of the same cell.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_signaling#cite_note-2) Juxtacrine signaling occurs between physically adjacent cells.[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_signaling#cite_note-3) Paracrine signaling occurs between nearby cells. Endocrine interaction occurs between distant cells, with the chemical signal usually carried by the blood.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_signaling#cite_note-4)

[Receptors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Receptor_(biochemistry) "Receptor (biochemistry)") are complex proteins or tightly bound multimer of proteins, located in the plasma membrane or within the interior of the cell such as in the [cytoplasm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cytoplasm "Cytoplasm"), [organelles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Organelle "Organelle"), and [nucleus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_nucleus "Cell nucleus"). Receptors have the ability to detect a signal either by binding to a specific chemical or by undergoing a conformational change when interacting with physical agents. It is the specificity of the chemical interaction between a given ligand and its receptor that confers the ability to trigger a specific cellular response. Receptors can be broadly classified into cell membrane receptors and intracellular receptors.

[![Image 5](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Cell_Signaling_Reception.jpg/250px-Cell_Signaling_Reception.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Cell_Signaling_Reception.jpg)

Diagram of G-protein coupled reception

[Cell membrane receptors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_surface_receptor "Cell surface receptor") can be further classified into ion channel linked receptors, G-Protein coupled receptors and enzyme linked receptors.

*   Ion channels receptors are large transmembrane proteins with a ligand activated gate function. When these receptors are activated, they may allow or block passage of specific ions across the cell membrane. Most receptors activated by physical stimuli such as pressure or temperature belongs to this category.
*   [G-protein receptors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/G_protein-coupled_receptor "G protein-coupled receptor") are multimeric proteins embedded within the plasma membrane. These receptors have extracellular, trans-membrane and intracellular [domains](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protein_domain "Protein domain"). The extracellular domain is responsible for the interaction with a specific ligand. The intracellular domain is responsible for the initiation of a cascade of chemical reactions which ultimately triggers the specific cellular function controlled by the receptor.
*   [Enzyme-linked receptors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enzyme-linked_receptor "Enzyme-linked receptor") are transmembrane proteins with an extracellular domain responsible for binding a specific ligand and an intracellular domain with enzymatic or catalytic activity. Upon activation the enzymatic portion is responsible for promoting specific intracellular chemical reactions.

[Intracellular receptors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intracellular_receptor "Intracellular receptor") have a different [mechanism of action](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mechanism_of_action "Mechanism of action"). They usually bind to lipid soluble ligands that diffuse passively through the plasma membrane such as steroid hormones. These ligands bind to specific cytoplasmic transporters that shuttle the hormone-transporter complex inside the nucleus where specific genes are activated and the synthesis of specific proteins is promoted.

The effector component of the signaling pathway begins with [signal transduction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Signal_transduction "Signal transduction"). In this process, the signal, by interacting with the receptor, starts a series of molecular events within the cell leading to the final effect of the signaling process. Typically the final effect consists in the activation of an ion channel ([ligand-gated ion channel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ligand-gated_ion_channel "Ligand-gated ion channel")) or the initiation of a [second messenger system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second_messenger_system "Second messenger system") cascade that propagates the signal through the cell. Second messenger systems can amplify or modulate a signal, in which activation of a few receptors results in multiple secondary messengers being activated, thereby amplifying the initial signal (the first messenger). The [downstream effects](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Upstream_and_downstream_(transduction) "Upstream and downstream (transduction)") of these signaling pathways may include additional enzymatic activities such as [proteolytic cleavage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protease "Protease"), [phosphorylation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phosphorylation "Phosphorylation"), [methylation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Methylation "Methylation"), and [ubiquitinylation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ubiquitinylation "Ubiquitinylation").

Signaling molecules can be synthesized from various biosynthetic pathways and released through [passive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Passive_transport "Passive transport") or [active transports](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Active_transport "Active transport"), or even from [cell damage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_damage "Cell damage").

Each cell is programmed to respond to specific extracellular signal molecules, and is the basis of [development](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developmental_biology "Developmental biology"), [tissue repair](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tissue_repair "Tissue repair"), [immunity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Immunity_(medical) "Immunity (medical)"), and [homeostasis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homeostasis "Homeostasis"). Errors in signaling interactions may cause diseases such as [cancer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cancer "Cancer"), [autoimmunity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Autoimmunity "Autoimmunity"), and [diabetes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diabetes "Diabetes").

Taxonomic range
---------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Cell_signaling&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Taxonomic range")]

In many small organisms such as [bacteria](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria"), [quorum sensing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quorum_sensing "Quorum sensing") enables individuals to begin an activity only when the population is sufficiently large. This signaling between cells was first observed in the marine bacterium _[Aliivibrio fischeri](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aliivibrio\_fischeri "Aliivibrio fischeri")_, which [produces light](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bioluminescence "Bioluminescence") when the population is dense enough.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_signaling#cite_note-Nealson-et-al-1970-5) The mechanism involves the production and detection of a signaling molecule, and the regulation of gene transcription in response. Quorum sensing operates in both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, and both within and between species.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_signaling#cite_note-Bassler-1999-6)

In [slime molds](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Slime_mold "Slime mold"), individual cells aggregate together to form fruiting bodies and eventually spores, under the influence of a chemical signal, known as an [acrasin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acrasin "Acrasin"). The individuals move by [chemotaxis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemotaxis "Chemotaxis"), i.e. they are attracted by the chemical gradient. Some species use [cyclic AMP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cyclic_AMP "Cyclic AMP") as the signal; others such as _[Polysphondylium violaceum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polysphondylium\_violaceum "Polysphondylium violaceum")_ use a [dipeptide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dipeptide "Dipeptide") known as [glorin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dipeptide#Other_dipeptides "Dipeptide").[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_signaling#cite_note-7)

In plants and animals, signaling between cells occurs either through release into the [extracellular space](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Extracellular_space "Extracellular space"), divided in paracrine signaling (over short distances) and endocrine signaling (over long distances), or by direct contact, known as juxtacrine signaling such as [notch signaling](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Notch_signaling "Notch signaling").[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_signaling#cite_note-8) Autocrine signaling is a special case of paracrine signaling where the secreting cell has the ability to respond to the secreted signaling molecule.[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_signaling#cite_note-bruce-9)[Synaptic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Synapse "Synapse") signaling is a special case of paracrine signaling (for [chemical synapses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemical_synapse "Chemical synapse")) or juxtacrine signaling (for [electrical synapses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electrical_synapse "Electrical synapse")) between [neurons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neuron "Neuron") and target cells.

Extracellular signal
--------------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Cell_signaling&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Extracellular signal")]

### Synthesis and release

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Cell_signaling&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Synthesis and release")]

[![Image 6](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Cell_signalling.png/500px-Cell_signalling.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Cell_signalling.png)

Different types of extracellular signaling

Many cell signals are carried by molecules that are released by one cell and move to make contact with another cell. Signaling molecules can belong to several chemical classes: [lipids](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lipid "Lipid"), [phospholipids](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phospholipid "Phospholipid"), [amino acids](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid"), [monoamines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monoamine "Monoamine"), [proteins](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protein "Protein"), [glycoproteins](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glycoprotein "Glycoprotein"), or [gases](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaseous_signaling_molecules "Gaseous signaling molecules"). Signaling molecules binding surface receptors are generally large and [hydrophilic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydrophilic "Hydrophilic") (e.g. [TRH](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TRH "TRH"), [Vasopressin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vasopressin "Vasopressin"), [Acetylcholine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acetylcholine "Acetylcholine")), while those entering the cell are generally small and [hydrophobic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydrophobic "Hydrophobic") (e.g. [glucocorticoids](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glucocorticoid "Glucocorticoid"), [thyroid hormones](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thyroid_hormone "Thyroid hormone"), [cholecalciferol](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cholecalciferol "Cholecalciferol"), [retinoic acid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Retinoic_acid "Retinoic acid")), but important exceptions to both are numerous, and the same molecule can act both via surface receptors or in an intracrine manner to different effects.[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_signaling#cite_note-bruce-9) In animal cells, specialized cells release these hormones and send them through the circulatory system to other parts of the body. They then reach target cells, which can recognize and respond to the hormones and produce a result. This is also known as endocrine signaling. Plant growth regulators, or plant hormones, move through cells or by diffusing through the air as a gas to reach their targets.[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_signaling#cite_note-Reece-10)[Hydrogen sulfide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydrogen_sulfide#Function_in_the_body "Hydrogen sulfide") is produced in small amounts by some cells of the human body and has a number of biological signaling functions. Only two other such gases are currently known to act as signaling molecules in the human body: [nitric oxide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biological_functions_of_nitric_oxide "Biological functions of nitric oxide") and [carbon monoxide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_monoxide#Physiology "Carbon monoxide").[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_signaling#cite_note-11)

### Transcript:
Intro To make a multicellular organism, cells must
be able to communicate with one another, and to do it cells often send out tiny chemical
signals that act on the receptors on other cells. Signaling distance Signals can be classified according to the
distance between the signaling cell and the target cell. Autocrine signals are produced by a cell and
go to its own receptors, so the cell sends a signal to itself. Paracrine signals are produced by a cell and
go to target cells that are nearby. And endocrine signals are produced by a cell
and go to target cells that are further away. Examples of these include hormones that are
secreted into the bloodstream, as well as cytokines that can be released at the site
of injury and act on the brain to cause a fever. Hydrophobic vs hydrophilic Signaling molecules or ligands can be hydrophobic,
meaning that they tend to repel water, or hydrophilic, meaning that they tend to stay
in water. Hydrophobic signalling molecules can’t freely
float in the extracellular space, so they’re brought to the target cells by carrier proteins. Hydrophobic molecules can diffuse across the
cell membrane and bind to receptor proteins inside the target cell - either in the cytoplasm
or in the nucleus. Most signal molecules are hydrophilic, so
they can freely float in the extracellular space to reach the target cells, but are then
unable to cross the cell membrane. So to pass on the signal, hydrophilic molecules
bind to receptors on the cell surface. These receptors are transmembrane proteins,
with an extracellular end that binds to the ligand, and an intracellular end that triggers
a signaling pathway inside the cell. Cell signaling pathway We can think of the cell signaling pathway
in three stages. The first stage is reception, which is when
the target cell’s receptor binds to a ligand. It’s like a key fitting into a lock. Then there’s transduction, which means that
the receptor protein changes in some way and that activates intracellular molecules - the
second messengers. The third stage is the cell’s response to
the signal. Zooming into these transmembrane receptors,
there are three major classes: G protein coupled receptors, enzyme-coupled receptors, and ion
channel receptors. G-protein coupled receptors are seven pass
transmembrane receptors. Gproteincoupled receptors These are really long proteins that have one
end that sits outside the cell and binds the ligand, then the snake-like protein dips in
and out of the cell membrane seven times, and finally ends on the inside of the cell. The end of the G-protein coupled receptor
that’s within the cell activates intracellular proteins called guanine nucleotide-binding
proteins or G proteins. G proteins are made up of three subunits called
alpha, beta, and gamma, sort of like a flower with three petals. The alpha and the gamma subunits are anchored
to the cell membrane and keep the G protein right next to the receptor. G proteins bind to guanosine diphosphate or
GDP when they’re inactive. When the alpha subunit is bound to GDP, the
three subunits stay together, so the flower is closed. But when the ligand binds, the G-protein coupled
receptor changes its shape, and this allows the G protein to release GDP and bind GTP
instead, activating the protein. When the alpha subunit is bound to GTP, the
alpha subunit separates from the beta and gamma subunits, like one petal opening and
separating from the others. When that happens, the alpha subunit is free
to interact with other proteins - it stimulates some while inhibiting others. But, to act on other proteins, the alpha subunit
turns GTP into GDP, and when that happens the three subunits come together again - the
flower closes - and the G protein is turned off. Overall, there are three types of G proteins:
Gq, Gi, and Gs, and each one stimulates and inhibits a different set of enzymes and molecular
pathways. GQ protein The Gq protein activates the enzyme phospholipase
C, which is found in the cell membrane. Phospholipase C then cleaves a phospholipid
called phosphatidylinositol 4,5-bisphosphate into inositol trisphosphate and diacylglycerol. Inositol trisphosphate is soluble and diffuses
freely through the cytoplasm and into the endoplasmic reticulum where it opens up calcium
channels. Since the calcium concentration is higher
in the endoplasmic reticulum than in the cytoplasm, calcium flows out of the endoplasmic reticulum
to the cytoplasm. The increased calcium concentration in the
cytoplasm changes the electrical charge of the cell and can lead to depolarization. Meanwhile, diacylglycerol remains attached
to the cell membrane and binds to the enzyme protein kinase C, which also relies on calcium
to fully activate. Once calcium levels in the cell go up, protein
kinase C starts to activate proteins by adding phosphoryl groups to them. Protein GS Next is protein Gs which stimulates the enzyme
adenylate cyclase. Activated adenylate cyclase takes adenosine
triphosphate or ATP, and removes two phosphate molecules transforming it into cyclic adenosine
monophosphate or cAMP. cAMP moves throughout the cytoplasm and binds to the enzyme protein
kinase A. Protein kinase A has two parts - a regulatory subunit and a catalytic subunit,
and cAMP specifically binds the regulatory subunit of protein kinase A. When cAMP binds
it makes the regulatory subunit dissociate from the catalytic subunit of protein kinase
A. It’s like pulling the pin out of the fire extinguisher allowing it, in this case
the catalytic subunit, to do its job. So after dissociating, the catalytic subunit
of protein kinase A is free to phosphorylate target proteins that trigger a cellular response. Finally, there’s the protein Gi, which is
also bound to adenylate cyclase - but in this Protein GI case, inhibits it, causing negative feedback
on protein Gs. This is particularly important in helping
to inactivate cells. Enzyme Coupled receptors Next are the enzyme-coupled receptors. They’re usually single-pass transmembrane
proteins, meaning that they have only one transmembrane segment, and their intracellular
end has intrinsic enzyme activity. In other words, enzyme-coupled receptors have
two parts - one domain is the receptor and the other domain is an enzyme. Each domain has a separate function, like
a swiss army knife composed of both a knife and scissors. The enzymatic domain is usually a protein
kinase that phosphorylates the receptor domain. Now, there are three main types of enzyme-coupled
receptors, based on the amino acid the receptors Receptor tyrosine kinases get phosphorylated at. The first group are the receptor tyrosine
kinases. These are the most common enzyme-coupled receptors,
and there are many subfamilies. Receptor tyrosine kinase are generally molecules
that can’t phosphorylate their own tyrosine side chains. When a ligand binds, two receptor chains come
together and dimerize, and they cross-phosphorylate one another at multiple tyrosine residues. This triggers a conformational change that
creates high-affinity binding sites for the second messengers, which can also be phosphorylated
and activated, triggering the signaling pathway. Next, are the tyrosine kinase associated receptors
which work in nearly the same way as receptor nacks tyrosine kinases, and their name even sounds
almost the same. The key difference is that they have no intrinsic
enzyme activity. Instead they’re associated with cytoplasmic
tyrosine kinases. When the receptors bind their ligand, the
cytoplasmic tyrosine kinases phosphorylate various target proteins to relay the signal. Finally, there are the receptor serine/threonine
kinases and they have a serine/threonine kinase domain on their intracellular end. There are two classes of these receptor serine/threonine
kinases - type I and type II - which are structurally similar. Ligand binding brings the two together together
so that the type II receptor can phosphorylate and activate the type I receptor, which in
turn recruits and phosphorylates various target proteins to relay the signal. Finally, there are the ion channel receptors
which are generally closed, but then open Ion channel up once they bind a specific ligand. They allow ions like chloride, calcium, sodium,
and potassium to passively flow down their gradient. This leads to a shift in electric charge distribution
inside the cell, triggering a cellular response. Alright, as a quick recap, autocrine signals
target the same cell, paracrine signals target Recap nearby cells, and endocrine signals target
distant cells. Hydrophobic ligands are able to diffuse across
the cell membrane and bind to receptor proteins inside the target cell. Hydrophilic ligands are unable to cross the
cell membrane, so they must bind to transmembrane receptors, which have an intracellular end
that triggers a signaling pathway inside the target cell. There are three major transmembrane receptor
classes: G protein coupled receptors, enzyme-coupled receptors, and ion channel receptors.
